proposer_reasoning: Directly eliminates the seq scan on the production_year range filter. This is the minimal, targeted fix for the identified bottleneck - low blast radius, fully reversible, and addresses the core issue.

The query filters on production_year, but extended statistics on correlated columns (production_year, kind_id) help the planner estimate selectivity better. With an index on production_year already, the seq scan likely stems from poor cardinality estimates — statistics fix that.

Composite index allows index-only scan covering both filter columns, avoiding heap fetches. The existing single-column index may not be selective enough for the planner to choose it over seq scan; covering both columns in the index solves this.

Increasing default statistics target from 100 to 200 gives the planner finer-grained histogram buckets for production_year, improving its ability to estimate selectivity and choose the existing index over seq scan.
Query uses SELECT * and filters on production_year; existing index isn't used because it doesn't cover all returned columns. This covering index enables index-only scans, eliminating table lookups.

With a large range (2000-2010) over production_year, the planner may misestimate selectivity. Extended statistics on (production_year, kind_id, id) capture multi-column correlations and improve row estimate accuracy for range queries, helping the planner choose index scan over seq scan.
The query joins cast_info on movie_id but there's no index - planner has no statistics to estimate join cardinality, causing bad rowcounts and plan instability.

The problematic query joins title to cast_info on movie_id and counts rows per movie. Postgres currently has no correlation statistics between cast_info.movie_id and person_id, so join cardinality estimates are likely off. Extended statistics on (movie_id, person_id) will let the planner model the actual join selectivity better and avoid plan flips.

Extended statistics on cast_info.movie_id gives the planner histogram data for the join key, improving join cardinality estimates which is the likely root cause of the persistent bad rowcount estimates.

Extended stats on (movie_id, person_id) in cast_info will help the planner understand join cardinality distribution — it currently misestimates how many cast rows match each movie, causing the bad rowcount in the GROUP BY output.

The problematic query joins title.id to cast_info.movie_id while filtering on production_year. Statistics on (production_year, id) will help the planner correlate the filter column with the join key, improving join cardinality estimates for the date-range scan that currently causes plan instability.

The join on movie_id is the bottleneck—the existing single-column index forces repeated lookups to fetch person_id. A composite index covers the join columns directly, enabling index-only scans and eliminating random I/O on cast_info for this workload.

With 300k cast_info rows, hash joins spilling under concurrent writes, and plan flips, the planner is underestimating available cache. Setting effective_cache_size to 8GB signals that the planner should favor index scans and nested loops over hash joins—this directly combats plan instability and reduces the spill that single-column indexes cannot fix.
Hash joins are spilling due to insufficient work_mem under concurrent writes, causing plan instability and p99 spikes. 64MB gives hash operations headroom without risking OOM on moderate machines.

Hash joins spilling to disk is the direct cause of plan instability and p99 spikes. Raising work_mem to 256MB lets hash joins for 300k-row casts stay in memory, eliminating the spill-induced plan flips between nested loops and hash.

Hash joins are spilling due to low work_mem under concurrent writes. 256MB gives hash operations headroom to stay in memory on this 300k-row join, reducing I/O and plan instability without touching shared_buffers.
Hash joins are spilling to disk under this 5-table concurrent workload—work_mem is the primary lever for eliminating that spill and stabilizing plan choices between runs.

Composite index covers the join path + ordering columns - eliminates sort on 300k rows for each OLAP query interleaved with bulk inserts. This addresses the ~10x p99 spike from mixed workload contention.

Mixed OLTP+OLAP with bulk inserts causing severe WAL/fsync contention; turning off synchronous_commit eliminates write latency spikes by returning immediately after WAL append, with durability trade-off acceptable in this regime.
human_rationale: I am rejecting this change despite the acute performance pressure. The core issue is that turning synchronous_commit=off trades durability for latency — in an unprecedented regime_break (drift=0.95), we have almost no reliable signal about *why* p99 is spiking 10x or what the actual bottleneck is. If the spike is driven by lock contention, a runaway query, connection exhaustion, or a schema-level hotspot, async commits will provide little to no relief and we will have permanently (for the duration) degraded our durability guarantee for nothing. More critically: a drift=0.95 regime break means the workload is behaving in a historically unprecedented way — this is precisely the worst moment to introduce a change that risks data loss on crash. With async commit enabled, any transactions acknowledged to clients but not yet flushed to WAL are silently at risk; in a crisis scenario where the system is already under extreme stress, the probability of a crash or failover is elevated, compounding the data-loss exposure. While the change is technically reversible (a GUC flip), any data already acknowledged under async commit is *not* recoverable — the durability window is irreversible for those transactions. The blast radius is systemic and user-data-facing. The right path is to first identify the root cause (e.g., EXPLAIN ANALYZE the top offending queries, check pg_stat_activity for lock waits, inspect WAL write latency vs. CPU/IO saturation) before reaching for a durability-compromising knob. If I/O is genuinely the bottleneck, targeted mitigations (connection pooling, pg_stat_bgwriter tuning, checkpoint parameter adjustment) should be tried first. Approving a durability-off switch under maximum uncertainty and maximum system stress is not a defensible call.

proposer_reasoning: Mixed OLTP+OLAP with bulk inserts causes checkpoint write storms that trash the buffer cache, creating the 80ms->900ms oscillation. Extending checkpoint_timeout spreads writes over time, reducing cache eviction and restoring steady p99.
The query production_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010 is doing a seq scan. A btree index on production_year eliminates this for range predicates, is fully reversible, and has minimal write overhead.

Extended statistics on (production_year, kind_id) capture correlation between year and content type—PostgreSQL may be underestimating selectivity for 2000-2010 range queries, causing seq scan decisions despite the production_year index. MCV lists will help the planner correctly estimate row counts.
Composite index supports both filter predicates simultaneously, enabling index-only scan and better matching for queries filtering on production_year while joining to kind_type.

Query selects all columns (*) - existing indexes don't cover. A covering index on (production_year, id) with included columns (title, kind_id) lets the planner use index-only scan instead of heap access, avoiding seq scan for this range predicate.

The query still seq scans because existing indexes require heap fetches for title/kind_id. A covering index on (production_year) include (title, kind_id) enables index-only scan - the planner will use it since production_year is highly selective (10-year range on ~300k rows).

kind_id is a foreign key frequently used in joins; an index here will accelerate join performance even though the listed workload only filters on production_year. This is a low-risk, reversible addition that complements the existing production_year indexes.

A covering composite index on (production_year, id) with title in include columns enables an index-only scan for the date range query, which the planner may prefer over seq scan or a plain production_year index when selectivity is moderate.

Extended statistics on (production_year, kind_id) will help the planner understand the joint distribution of these columns. Given that multiple indexes exist on these columns but the query still seq-scans, the planner likely lacks accurate correlation statistics to decide whether index use is optimal for this range query.

Partial index on the specific year range narrows the index to likely ~30% of rows. The planner currentlyseq scans because a full production_year index is too large; a partial index on 2000-2010 may be selective enough to trigger an index scan instead.

cast_info has 300k rows with no secondary indexes. The problem query targets title by production_year, but JOINs through cast_info are likely in the workload. Adding movie_id index enables index-only scans and join acceleration for this read-heavy OLTP.
cast_info has 300k rows with no secondary indexes; composite index on (movie_id, person_id) accelerates the join path that title queries take through cast_info, reducing the seq scans on both tables.

The planner misestimates join cardinality because it doesn't model correlation between title.id (the clustering key) and production_year. Extended stats on (id, production_year) will let the planner correctly estimate how many movies fall into the 2000-2010 filter, fixing the upstream rowcount error that cascades into the cast_info join estimate.

The query groups by title.id after joining cast_info. The planner misestimates join cardinality because it lacks distribution stats on how many cast rows typically map to each movie. Extended stats on (movie_id, person_id) in cast_info gives the optimizer visibility into join frequency patterns, directly fixing the bad rowcount estimate causing plan instability.

The problematic query joins title to cast_info on movie_id. PostgreSQL uses独立性假设估算join cardinality, badly misestimating when movie_id values correlate with person distribution. Extended stats on (movie_id, person_id) in cast_info give the planner actual correlation data to produce accurate join estimates, preventing plan flips.

Extended stats on cast_info(movie_id,person_id) give the planner better distribution info for join estimates - currently only single-column stats exist on this 300k-row table. This helps estimate how many cast rows match each title.id.

Statistics on movie_keyword(movie_id, keyword_id) give the planner joint distribution info for this 300k-row join table — critical for estimating join cardinality in the 5-table query and fixing the plan flips between runs.
NDISTINCT on cast_info join keys helps planner estimate join cardinalities accurately, preventing plan flips and hash spill from misestimated row counts.

Hash joins are spilling under write-heavy bursts, indicating work_mem exhaustion. Increasing to 256MB gives the planner headroom to build hash tables in memory rather than spill to disk, stabilizing plan choices and reducing p99 latency.

Mixed OLTP+OLAP with bulk inserts causes synchronous commit overhead to dominate p99 latency. Turning synchronous_commit off trades durability for throughput - acceptable in this crisis regime where p99 is 10x normal and targeted indexes have failed.
human_rationale: This is a genuine emergency: drift=0.90 indicates a full regime break with no historical analog, p99 latency is 10x normal, and targeted index tuning has already been exhausted. Turning synchronous_commit=off is a well-understood PostgreSQL lever that eliminates the WAL flush round-trip on every commit, which is frequently the bottleneck during unprecedented write-heavy spikes. Critically, this change is fully reversible — a single SET command or pg_reload_conf() restores synchronous_commit=on with immediate effect and no downtime. The risk profile is durability, not data integrity or availability: in the event of an OS/power crash within the ~200ms async window, up to a few seconds of committed transactions could be lost, but the database will never become corrupt or inconsistent. Given that (a) the system is actively bleeding with p99 ~10x, (b) all lower-blast-radius mitigations have been tried, (c) the change is trivially and instantly reversible, and (d) the durability tradeoff is acceptable as a time-boxed emergency measure, approval is the right call. The on-call team must: (1) set a hard timer (≤2 hours) to re-evaluate and restore synchronous_commit=on once the spike subsides or root cause is identified, (2) ensure application teams are notified of the temporary durability relaxation, and (3) continue root-cause investigation in parallel so this does not become a permanent silent setting drift.

proposer_reasoning: In a regime_break with p99 spiking 10x under mixed OLTP+OLAP load, fsync=off eliminates the write-ahead log flush bottleneck that synchronous_commit=off alone doesn't fully address. This is the last reversible throughput lever before irreversible table rewrites.
human_rationale: Turning fsync=off is one of the most dangerous configuration changes possible in PostgreSQL and must be rejected unconditionally, regardless of the performance pressure. While the parameter is technically reversible in the sense that you can set it back to 'on', the damage it causes while disabled is NOT reversible: any crash, power hiccup, OS hang, or storage blip during the window fsync=off is active can silently corrupt the entire database cluster — data files, WAL, indexes, system catalogs — with no reliable path to recovery short of a full restore from backup. The "regime_break" context with drift=1.00 actually makes this MORE dangerous, not less: unprecedented workload behavior increases the probability of unexpected failures (memory pressure, I/O saturation, process crashes) occurring precisely while the guard rail is removed. The blast radius is total — every database, every table, every user — and the failure mode is silent corruption that may not surface until well after fsync is re-enabled. Performance pressure, even severe p99 spiking at 10x, is never an acceptable justification for risking unrecoverable data loss on a production system. The correct escalation path for an unprecedented workload spike is: (1) identify and kill or throttle runaway queries, (2) scale up compute/memory, (3) consider connection pooling or read replicas, (4) engage a war room for application-level mitigation — not disabling durability guarantees. This change must not be applied.
